preamble: The shift from the voiced velar fricative [ɣ] to the voiceless glottal fricative [h] involves both debuccalization and devoicing. The oral constriction at the velum is lost, and vocal fold vibration ceases, leaving only glottal friction. This is a significant weakening process found in various Germanic and Slavic dialects.

examples.note: Common weakening of the velar fricative in unstressed positions or specific dialectal developments.
Historical Slavic *g became a voiced glottal fricative [ɦ] in these languages, a process involving debuccalization. (Note: [h] and [ɦ] are closely related outcomes).

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Lass, R. (1997). Historical Linguistics and Language Change.
